behaviour.mcq: Select one option. Answer revealed immediately on selection.

questions.stem: Seborrhoeic dermatitis is linked to overgrowth of which organism?
questions.explanation: Seborrhoeic dermatitis (greasy scale of scalp, eyebrows, nasolabial folds, chest) is associated with commensal Malassezia; it is more severe/extensive in Parkinson’s disease and HIV. Treat with antifungal shampoos ± mild steroid.

questions.stem: Hidradenitis suppurativa characteristically affects:
questions.explanation: HS = chronic follicular occlusion in apocrine-bearing intertriginous sites → painful nodules, sinus tracts and scarring (staged by Hurley). Associated with smoking, obesity and metabolic syndrome.

questions.stem: The Fitzpatrick classification is used to grade:
questions.explanation: The Fitzpatrick phototypes (I–VI) grade constitutive pigmentation and tanning/burning tendency; lower types (I–II) burn easily and carry higher skin-cancer risk.

questions.stem: The ash-leaf macule, adenoma sebaceum (facial angiofibromas) and periungual (Koenen) fibromas are features of:
questions.explanation: Tuberous sclerosis: hypopigmented ash-leaf macules, facial angiofibromas, shagreen patch and periungual fibromas, with CNS/renal involvement. (Café-au-lait macules and axillary freckling belong to NF1.)

questions.stem: Axillary freckling (Crowe’s sign) and multiple café-au-lait macules point to:
questions.explanation: NF1: ≥6 café-au-lait macules, axillary/inguinal freckling (Crowe’s sign), cutaneous neurofibromas and Lisch nodules of the iris. Autosomal dominant, chromosome 17 (neurofibromin).

questions.stem: Xeroderma pigmentosum predisposes to multiple early skin cancers because of a defect in:
questions.explanation: XP = inherited failure of nucleotide-excision repair of UV-induced DNA damage → extreme photosensitivity and numerous cutaneous malignancies from childhood. Strict photoprotection is essential.

questions.stem: Which nail sign is associated with iron-deficiency anaemia?
questions.explanation: Koilonychia (concave “spoon” nails) is a classic sign of chronic iron deficiency. Pitting/oil-drop = psoriasis; splinter haemorrhages = trauma or infective endocarditis.

questions.stem: Transverse ridges across the nail plate reflecting a temporary halt in growth (e.g. after severe illness) are called:
questions.explanation: Beau’s lines are transverse grooves from temporary arrest of nail matrix growth during systemic illness, high fever or chemotherapy; their position dates the insult.

questions.stem: A fixed drug eruption is best characterised by:

options.A: A round dusky plaque recurring at the same site with each drug exposure

questions.explanation: A fixed drug eruption recurs at the same site(s) on re-exposure to the culprit drug (often sulfonamides, NSAIDs, tetracyclines), leaving post-inflammatory pigmentation.

questions.stem: DRESS syndrome (drug reaction with eosinophilia and systemic symptoms) typically features:

options.A: Rash, fever, eosinophilia and internal-organ (e.g. hepatic) involvement

questions.explanation: DRESS = a severe delayed reaction (anticonvulsants, allopurinol, sulfonamides) with widespread rash, fever, facial oedema, eosinophilia and organ involvement (liver, kidney). Onset is often 2–6 weeks after starting the drug.

questions.stem: Tinea capitis is best diagnosed/managed by:

options.B: KOH microscopy/culture and ORAL antifungal therapy

questions.explanation: Scalp ringworm needs systemic (oral) antifungals (e.g. terbinafine, griseofulvin) because topical agents cannot penetrate the follicle; confirm with KOH/culture. Some Microsporum species fluoresce green on Wood’s light.

questions.stem: Which describes the KOH “spaghetti-and-meatballs” appearance?

options.A: Short hyphae with clusters of spores in pityriasis versicolor

questions.explanation: In pityriasis versicolor, KOH of scale shows short curved hyphae plus round spores — the classic “spaghetti-and-meatballs.” (Multinucleate giant cells on a Tzanck smear indicate herpes.)

questions.stem: A Tzanck smear showing multinucleated giant cells supports a diagnosis of:
questions.explanation: The Tzanck smear from a blister base shows multinucleated giant cells in herpetic infections (HSV, VZV). It is a rapid bedside test, now often replaced by PCR.

questions.stem: Condylomata lata (broad, moist, wart-like papules) are a manifestation of:
questions.explanation: Condylomata lata are highly infectious moist papules of secondary syphilis; do not confuse them with condylomata acuminata (HPV genital warts). Secondary syphilis also causes a rash involving the palms and soles.

questions.stem: Which is TRUE of the rash of secondary syphilis?

options.A: It classically involves the palms and soles

questions.explanation: Secondary syphilis produces a widespread copper-coloured maculopapular rash that characteristically involves the palms and soles, often with mucous patches, lymphadenopathy and condylomata lata.

questions.stem: Lupus pernio, a violaceous indurated plaque of the nose/cheeks, is a cutaneous marker of:
questions.explanation: Despite the name, lupus pernio is a specific sign of sarcoidosis (not lupus), associated with chronic disease and upper-respiratory involvement.

questions.stem: Which vascular lesion is caused by human herpesvirus-8 and is an AIDS-defining tumour?
questions.explanation: Kaposi sarcoma (HHV-8) presents as violaceous patches/plaques/nodules; the aggressive epidemic form is AIDS-defining. Cherry (Campbell de Morgan) angiomas are trivial benign papules.

questions.stem: Erythema multiforme is most classically triggered by:
questions.explanation: EM = symmetrical target lesions on the extremities, most often triggered by HSV (also Mycoplasma, drugs). It is distinct from SJS/TEN, which are predominantly drug-induced with prominent mucosal necrosis.

questions.stem: The most appropriate long-term management of chronic plaque psoriasis with 5% body-surface involvement is initially:
questions.explanation: Limited plaque psoriasis is managed first with topical corticosteroid plus a vitamin-D analogue (e.g. calcipotriol); phototherapy, then systemic/biologic agents are escalated for extensive or resistant disease.

questions.stem: Mohs micrographic surgery is particularly indicated for:

options.A: High-risk facial non-melanoma skin cancer where tissue sparing and margin control matter

questions.explanation: Mohs offers complete margin assessment with maximal tissue conservation — ideal for high-risk or recurrent BCC/SCC in cosmetically/functionally sensitive sites such as the face.

questions.stem: A photosensitising drug commonly implicated in drug-induced photosensitivity is:
questions.explanation: Tetracyclines (especially doxycycline), thiazides, amiodarone, fluoroquinolones and some NSAIDs cause photosensitivity. Patients should be counselled on photoprotection.

questions.stem: The mainstay of preventing UV-induced skin cancer and photoageing is:
questions.explanation: Broad-spectrum sunscreen (UVA+UVB) with photoprotective behaviour (shade, clothing, avoiding peak sun) is the cornerstone of preventing actinic damage, photoageing and skin cancer.

questions.stem: Narrowband UVB and PUVA phototherapy are used mainly for:
questions.explanation: Phototherapy (NB-UVB, or psoralen + UVA = PUVA) treats widespread inflammatory disease such as psoriasis, atopic eczema and vitiligo. Long-term PUVA increases skin-cancer risk.

questions.stem: Which feature suggests a melanocytic naevus is a benign “halo” (Sutton) naevus rather than melanoma?

options.A: A symmetric surrounding rim of depigmentation with a central naevus that regresses

questions.explanation: A halo (Sutton) naevus has a symmetrical depigmented rim from an immune response; the central naevus often involutes. It is benign, though any atypia warrants review.

questions.stem: Methotrexate, used in severe psoriasis, requires monitoring principally for:
questions.explanation: Methotrexate can cause hepatotoxicity, myelosuppression and pneumonitis; monitor FBC, LFTs and renal function, co-prescribe folic acid, and avoid in pregnancy (teratogenic).

questions.stem: The characteristic distribution of atopic dermatitis in older children and adults is:
questions.explanation: In infancy eczema favours the face/extensors, but from childhood onward it becomes flexural (elbow and knee creases, wrists, neck), with lichenification from chronic scratching.

questions.stem: Angioedema differs from urticaria mainly in that it:

options.A: Involves deeper dermal/subcutaneous swelling, often of lips/eyelids/airway

questions.explanation: Angioedema is deep dermal/subcutaneous swelling (lips, eyelids, tongue, larynx) that can threaten the airway; it may accompany urticaria or occur alone (e.g. ACE-inhibitor or hereditary C1-inhibitor deficiency).

questions.stem: Which of the following is a scarring (cicatricial) alopecia?
questions.explanation: Lichen planopilaris (and discoid lupus, folliculitis decalvans) destroy follicles → scarring alopecia with loss of follicular ostia. Alopecia areata, telogen effluvium and androgenetic alopecia are non-scarring.

questions.stem: Necrolytic migratory erythema, an eruption of migrating erosive plaques around the mouth and groin, is a clue to:
questions.explanation: Necrolytic migratory erythema is a paraneoplastic sign of a pancreatic glucagonoma (with weight loss, diabetes and anaemia) — an example of a skin sign of internal disease.

questions.stem: Acanthosis nigricans — velvety hyperpigmented thickening of the axillae and neck — is most often associated with:

options.A: Insulin resistance / obesity (and, if abrupt, internal malignancy)

questions.explanation: Acanthosis nigricans usually reflects insulin resistance/obesity; sudden extensive onset in a thin adult can be paraneoplastic (classically gastric adenocarcinoma). It is another skin marker of systemic disease.
